title: On a Unique Ergodicity of Some Markov Processes
overall_summary: The paper proves that a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup on a Polish space has at most one invariant probability measure if it satisfies the e-property and a weak topological irreducibility condition. The core argument is not strong Feller smoothing: it shows that, for e-processes, distinct ergodic invariant measures must have disjoint supports, while weak topological irreducibility forces supports of ergodic invariant measures to intersect. A central technical device is the notion that a point generates an invariant measure through weak limits of Cesaro averages Q_tδ_x, together with tightness, Arzela-Ascoli, and equicontinuity from the e-property. The paper also gives an SPDE criterion: for additive-noise evolution equations, weak dissipativity of the deterministic part implies the e-property via synchronous coupling, so uniqueness follows once weak irreducibility is verified. For the original SPDE, the main possible use is to reduce uniqueness of invariant measures to proving the e-property on the chosen state space and a weak support/irreducibility statement for the transition probabilities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1: tight Cesaro averages of an e-process have a unique weak limit
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a Polish space and let (P_t)_{t≥0} be a Markov semigroup on bounded Borel functions on X. Assume throughout that (P_t) is Feller, meaning P_t maps bounded continuous functions to bounded continuous functions for every t>0, and stochastically continuous, meaning P_tψ(x)→ψ(x) as t↓0 for every bounded continuous ψ and x∈X. Let π_t(x,A)=P_t1_A(x) be its transition probabilities and P_t^* its dual action on probability measures. For t>0 define Q_tμ:=t^{-1}∫_0^t P_s^*μ ds, set Q_0μ:=μ, and write Q_t(x,·):=Q_tδ_x. Let T be the set of points x∈X for which the family {Q_t(x,·):t≥0} is tight. Suppose (P_t) has the e-property: for every bounded Lipschitz function φ:X→R and every x∈X, lim_{y→x} sup_{t≥0}|P_tφ(x)-P_tφ(y)|=0. Then for every x∈T, the net/sequence Q_t(x,·), t→∞, converges weakly to a probability measure, and that limit is invariant for (P_t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix x∈T. By tightness and Prokhorov, every sequence t_n→∞ has weakly convergent subsequences; standard Krylov-Bogoliubov arguments using Feller and stochastic continuity make every weak subsequential limit invariant. To prove uniqueness of the subsequential limit, argue by contradiction. Suppose Q_{s_n}(x,·)→μ and Q_{t_n}(x,·)→ν weakly along s_n,t_n→∞ with μ≠ν. Choose f∈L_b(X), bounded Lipschitz, separating μ and ν, and choose ε>0 with |∫f dμ−∫f dν|>ε. Tightness gives a compact K with Q_t(x,K)>1−ε/(6∥f∥) for every t≥0; by the Alexandrov/Portmanteau theorem, μ(K) and ν(K) are also close to 1. Define averaged functions g_n:=s_n^{-1}∫_0^{s_n}P_s f ds. The e-property implies that {g_n} is equicontinuous on K and uniformly bounded, so Arzela-Ascoli gives a uniformly convergent subsequence on K, with limit ~f∈C(K). Extend ~f to f_*∈C(X) with ∥f_*∥≤∥f∥. The mass estimate outside K and uniform convergence on K give |∫f_* dμ−∫g_n dμ|<ε/3 for large n; since μ is invariant, ∫g_n dμ=∫f dμ. Thus ∫f_*dμ is close to ∫f dμ. Next choose N with ∫f_*dQ_{t_N}(x,·) close to ∫f_*dν. Applying the same compact/uniform convergence estimate to the fixed measure Q_{t_N}(x,·) gives ∫f_*dQ_{t_N}(x,·) close to ∫g_n dQ_{t_N}(x,·)=∫f dQ_{s_n,t_N}(x,·), where Q_{s,t}=Q_s∘Q_t. The proof then invokes Lemma 2 of Komorowski-Peszat-Szarek as cited in the paper, which says that for fixed t_N the total variation distance between Q_{s_n,t_N}(x,·) and Q_{s_n}(x,·) tends to 0 as n→∞. Hence this last integral is close to ∫f dμ. Combining the three estimates by the triangle inequality contradicts |∫f dμ−∫f dν|>ε. Therefore all subsequential weak limits coincide, and tightness gives full weak convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1: generated measures have the same Cesaro limit as the generating point
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same setting as Lemma 1, assume (P_t) has the e-property. Say that a probability measure μ on X is generated by a point x∈X if μ belongs to the weak closure of the convex hull of {Q_tδ_x:t≥0}. If x∈T and μ is generated by x, then Q_tμ has the same weak limit as Q_t(x,·) as t→∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix f∈L_b(X) with ∥f∥≤1. The paper uses Lemma 2 of Szarek-Slęczka-Urbański together with the e-property to obtain a uniform-in-time continuity statement: if μ_n→μ weakly, then sup_{t≥0}|∫f dQ_tμ_n−∫f dQ_tμ|→0. Since μ is generated by x, for any ε>0 one can choose finitely many times t_1,...,t_N and weights α_i≥0 with sum 1 such that the convex combination Σ_i α_i Q_{t_i}(x,·) approximates μ well enough that sup_{t≥0}|∫f dQ_tμ−∫f dQ_t(Σ_i α_iQ_{t_i}(x,·))|<ε/2. For each fixed t_i, Lemma 2 of Komorowski-Peszat-Szarek gives ∥Q_tQ_{t_i}(x,·)−Q_t(x,·)∥_{TV}→0 as t→∞. Thus for all large t, each term Q_tQ_{t_i}(x,·) is within ε/2 in total variation of Q_t(x,·), uniformly over the finite set i=1,...,N. Hence |∫f dQ_tμ−∫f dQ_t(x,·)|<ε for all large t. Lemma 1 supplies convergence of Q_t(x,·), so Q_tμ converges to the same limit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 1: local characterization of generation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same Polish-space Markov semigroup setting, assume the e-property. A probability measure μ is generated by x, meaning μ∈cl_w conv{Q_tδ_x:t≥0}, if and only if for every ε>0, μ belongs to the weak closure of the convex hull of all measures Q_tν where t≥0, ν is a probability measure, and supp ν⊂B(x,ε). Here B(x,ε) is an open ball for any compatible metric on the Polish space X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward direction is immediate by taking ν=δ_x. For the reverse direction, the key observation is the uniform-in-time consequence of the e-property: for each bounded Lipschitz f and tolerance η>0 there is a radius r>0 such that |∫f dQ_tν−∫f dQ_t(x,·)|<η for every t≥0 whenever ν is a probability measure supported in B(x,r). Therefore convex combinations of Q_tν with ν supported sufficiently close to x are weakly indistinguishable, on bounded Lipschitz test functions and uniformly in t, from corresponding convex combinations of Q_tδ_x. This transfers membership in all the local closed convex hulls to membership in cl_w conv{Q_tδ_x:t≥0}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2: every support point of an ergodic invariant measure generates it
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be Polish and let (P_t) be a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup with the e-property. Let μ be an ergodic invariant probability measure, where ergodic means that every μ-invariant measurable set has μ-measure 0 or 1. Then every point x∈supp μ generates μ, i.e. μ∈cl_w conv{Q_tδ_x:t≥0}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has three steps. Step I is an accessibility fact from support points. Fix x∈supp μ and an open neighbourhood A of x. Define B={y∈X:π_t(y,A)=0 for all t≥0}. Stochastic continuity permits B to be written as an intersection over rational times: B=∩_{q∈Q_+}{y:π_q(y,A)=0}. If μ(B)>0, then for z∈B and any t≥0 one must have π_t(z,B)=1: otherwise π_t(z,X\B)>0, so for some rational q_m the set C_m={y:π_{q_m}(y,A)>0} has positive π_t(z,·)-mass, implying π_{t+q_m}(z,A)>0, contrary to z∈B. Thus B is μ-invariant. Ergodicity would force μ(B)=1, but B⊂X\A and μ(A)>0 because x∈supp μ, contradiction. Hence μ(B)=0. Step II fixes ε>0 and A=B(x,ε), and proves that μ can be approximated in mass from below by finite sums of images of probability measures supported in A. More precisely, let γ be the supremum of ν(X) over all finite measures ν≤μ of the form ν=Σ_{i=1}^n α_i P_{t_i}^*ν_i, where ν_i are probability measures supported in A and α_i,t_i≥0. If γ<1, choose ν_n of this form with ν_n(X)→γ and let μ_n=μ−ν_n. A subsequence converges weakly to a nonzero μ_*. By Step I, for some z∈supp μ_*⊂supp μ and some t≥0, η:=π_t(z,A)>0. Feller implies lower semicontinuity of y↦π_t(y,A) for open A, so there is θ>0 with π_t(y,A)≥η/2 on B(z,θ). If α:=μ_*(B(z,θ))>0 and N is large, then μ_N(B(z,θ))>α/2 and γ−ν_N(X)<αη/4, so P_t^*μ_N(A)≥αη/4. Since μ=P_t^*μ≥P_t^*ν_N plus a nonzero normalized piece supported on A, one constructs a competitor in the definition of γ with mass at least ν_N(X)+αη/4, contradiction. Therefore γ=1. Step III converts the mass approximation into generation. For arbitrary ε>0 choose ν_i supported in B(x,ε), times t_i, and weights α_i with Σα_i>1−ε/2 and μ≥Σα_iP_{t_i}^*ν_i. Choose T large enough that ∥Q_Tν_i−Q_TP_{t_i}^*ν_i∥_{TV}<ε/2 for all i, using the fixed-shift Cesaro averaging lemma cited from Komorowski-Peszat-Szarek. Since μ is invariant, Q_Tμ=μ, and a total-variation triangle inequality gives ∥μ−Σα_iQ_Tν_i∥_{TV}<ε. Remark 1 then implies that μ is generated by x.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1: distinct ergodic invariant measures have disjoint supports
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be Polish and let (P_t) be a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup with the e-property. If μ and ν are two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures for (P_t), then supp μ∩supp ν=∅.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose x∈supp μ∩supp ν. The paper recalls from earlier work that the support of every invariant measure is contained in T, the set of points whose Cesaro averages {Q_t(x,·)} are tight; hence x∈T. By Proposition 2, because μ and ν are ergodic invariant measures and x lies in both supports, x generates both μ and ν. Proposition 1 then says that Q_tμ and Q_tν have the same weak limit as Q_t(x,·). But μ and ν are invariant, so Q_tμ=μ and Q_tν=ν for every t. Hence μ=ν, contradicting distinctness. Therefore the supports cannot intersect.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2: weak topological irreducibility plus e-property implies at most one invariant measure
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be Polish and let (P_t) be a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup with transition probabilities π_t. Assume the e-property. Assume also weak topological irreducibility: for every pair x_1,x_2∈X there exists a point y∈X such that, for every open neighbourhood A of y, there exist times t_1,t_2>0 with π_{t_i}(x_i,A)>0 for i=1,2. Then (P_t) has at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If there were two distinct invariant probability measures, standard ergodic-decomposition reasoning gives two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures μ and ν. Pick x_1∈supp μ and x_2∈supp ν. By weak topological irreducibility, there is y∈X such that every neighbourhood A of y is reachable with positive probability from x_1 and from x_2 at possibly different positive times. The Feller property implies lower semicontinuity of z↦π_t(z,A) for open A. Thus if π_t(x_1,A)>0 and x_1∈supp μ, then π_t(z,A)>0 on a neighbourhood of x_1 of positive μ-measure, and invariance gives μ(A)=∫π_t(z,A)μ(dz)>0. Hence y∈supp μ; similarly y∈supp ν. Therefore supp μ∩supp ν≠∅, contradicting Theorem 1. Thus at most one invariant probability measure exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2: weak dissipativity gives the e-property for additive-noise evolution equations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a Hilbert space with norm ∥·∥ and inner product ⟨·,·⟩. Consider a Markov process {Y_x(t)}_{t≥0}, x∈H, given as the unique mild solution of dY=(AY+F(Y))dt+dZ(t), Y_x(0)=x, where Z is an additive noise on H. Assume the process is Feller. Define G(y):=Ay+F(y). If G is weakly dissipative, meaning ⟨G(x)−G(y),x−y⟩≤0 for all x,y∈H, then the associated semigroup P_tf(x):=E f(Y_x(t)) has the e-property. In fact, for every Lipschitz f:H→R with Lipschitz constant L_f, |P_tf(x)−P_tf(y)|≤L_f∥x−y∥ for all x,y∈H and t≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Couple the two solutions Y_x and Y_y by driving them with the same additive noise Z. The noise cancels in the difference, so formally d(Y_x−Y_y)=(A(Y_x−Y_y)+F(Y_x)−F(Y_y))dt. Taking the squared norm and expectation gives (1/2)d/dt E∥Y_x(t)−Y_y(t)∥²≤E⟨G(Y_x(t))−G(Y_y(t)),Y_x(t)−Y_y(t)⟩≤0 by weak dissipativity. Therefore E∥Y_x(t)−Y_y(t)∥²≤∥x−y∥² for every t. For a Lipschitz f, |P_tf(x)−P_tf(y)|≤E|f(Y_x(t))−f(Y_y(t))|≤L_f(E∥Y_x(t)−Y_y(t)∥²)^{1/2}≤L_f∥x−y∥. This bound is uniform in t, which is exactly the e-property for bounded Lipschitz f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3: SPDE uniqueness criterion from weak dissipativity and weak irreducibility
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert-space evolution-equation setting of Lemma 2, suppose the semigroup associated with dY=(AY+F(Y))dt+dZ(t) is weakly topologically irreducible in the sense that for every x_1,x_2∈H there is y∈H such that every open neighbourhood of y is hit with positive probability from both x_1 and x_2 at some positive times. If the deterministic part G= A+F is weakly dissipative, then the semigroup admits at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Weak dissipativity implies the e-property by Lemma 2. The weak irreducibility assumption is exactly the irreducibility hypothesis of Theorem 2, and the process is assumed Feller in the Section 3 setup. Applying Theorem 2 yields at most one invariant probability measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Estimate (3.4): isometry for the impulsive-noise stochastic integral
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the paper's impulsive-noise setup, let ν be a finite Borel measure on R with supp ν=R and a_ν:=∫_R σ²ν(dσ)<∞. A measure-valued impulsive white noise Z is constructed from independent Poissonian impulse times, spatial locations in a partition of [0,1], and jump sizes with law ν restricted to pieces U_n satisfying ∫_{U_n}σν(dσ)=0. For one coordinate copy Z_k and a predictable L²([0,1])-valued integrand X, the stochastic integral satisfies, for 0≤t_1≤t_2<T, E|∫_{t_1}^{t_2} X(s)dZ_k(s)|² = a_ν E∫_{t_1}^{t_2}∥X(s)∥² ds. For predictable Hilbert-Schmidt-operator-valued X, the Hilbert-valued integral is defined by ∫_{t_1}^{t_2}X(s)dZ(s):=Σ_{n≥1}(∫_{t_1}^{t_2}(X(s)^*e_n)dZ_n(s))e_n, where {e_n} is the Dirichlet sine basis of L²([0,1]); the series converges in L²(Ω;L²) when E∫∥X(s)∥_{HS}²ds<∞ and yields a square-integrable cadlag martingale.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality is first checked for simple predictable random fields X(ω,s,·)=ξ(ω)1_{(a,b]}(s)1_A(·), with ξ bounded and F_a-measurable. The construction of the impulse process gives independent increments, the centering condition ∫_{U_n}σν(dσ)=0 removes cross terms, and the second moment of the compound Poisson sum contributes exactly ∫σ²ν(dσ) times the spacetime L² norm. Density of simple predictable fields in the predictable L² space extends the isometry to all integrands. For Hilbert-Schmidt X, independence of the coordinate noises Z_n and the isometry give Σ_n E(∫(X^*e_n)dZ_n)² = a_ν E∫Σ_n∥X^*e_n∥²ds = a_ν E∫∥X∥_{HS}²ds, proving convergence of the defining series. Stochastic continuity and Doob's theorem give a cadlag modification.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3: positive-probability control of one impulsive-noise integral
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Z be the scalar measure-valued impulsive white noise constructed in Section 3 from a finite full-support jump-size measure ν on R with finite second moment, spatial partition {O_m}, jump-size partition {U_n}, exponential impulse times, uniform spatial locations on O_m, and jump sizes distributed as ν conditioned on U_n. Let X∈L²([0,T];L²([0,1])) be such that ∫_s^t∫_0^1 |X(u)(x)| dx du>0 for some 0≤s<t≤T. Then for every a∈R and ε>0, P(|∫_s^t X(u)dZ(u)−a|<ε and sup_{s≤τ≤t}|∫_s^τ X(u)dZ(u)|<|a|+ε)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces to s=0 and a≠0. Since X is nonzero on a set of positive spacetime measure, choose a spatial partition cell O_m, a number v≠0, and a measurable set Γ⊂[0,t]×O_m of positive Lebesgue measure such that |X(u)(x)−v|<ε|v|/(3|a|) on Γ. Set γ=a/v and choose n with γ∈U_n. Choose η>0 smaller than both ε/(3|v|) and |a/v|. Consider the event Ω_0 that: no impulse from any pair (k,l)≠(n,m) occurs before t; the first impulse of type (n,m) has time and location in Γ; the second impulse of type (n,m) occurs after t; and the first jump size σ_1^{(n,m)} lies in (γ−η,γ+η). These four requirements concern independent random variables, so their probabilities multiply. The no-other-jump event has probability at least exp(−ν(R)t)>0; the time-location event has positive probability because Γ has positive Lebesgue measure and the density is positive on [0,∞)×O_m; the second waiting-time event is positive; and the jump-size event is positive by the full-support assumption on ν. On Ω_0 there is exactly one relevant jump in [0,t], located where X is close to v and with size close to γ, so the final integral is within ε of a, while all partial integrals are bounded by |a|+ε. Hence the desired event has positive probability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4: support property for the impulsive stochastic convolution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S(t) be the Dirichlet heat semigroup on L²([0,1]) with eigenbasis e_k(x)=√2 sin(πkx), and let Z be the Hilbert-valued impulsive noise built from independent scalar copies of the measure-valued impulsive noise. Define the stochastic convolution Z_S(t):=∫_0^t S(t−s)dZ(s). For every f∈L²([0,T];L²([0,1])), every g∈L²([0,1]), and every ε>0, P(∫_0^T∥f(t)−Z_S(t)∥²dt<ε and ∥Z_S(T)−g∥<ε)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First reduce by density to the case where f is a step function f=Σ_{j=1}^N f_j1_{[t_{j−1},t_j)} with 0=t_0<...<t_N=T, f_1=0, f_N=g, and all f_j lie in a fixed finite-dimensional span of sine modes. The stochastic-convolution isometry implies E∫_0^T∥Z_S(t)∥²dt<∞, hence its high Fourier-mode tail can be made smaller than ε with positive probability. Thus it suffices to show, for each fixed finite coordinate i, that the coordinate process ⟨Z_S(t),e_i⟩ can track the prescribed coordinate path with positive probability and end near the desired coordinate. For a fixed i, set a small tolerance ~ε=ε/T and refine the time partition so that the heat-semigroup decay over a mesh interval changes the target coordinate by at most ~ε/8, i.e. max_j |(1−e^{−π²i²δ})⟨f_j,e_i⟩|<~ε/8. The proof proceeds by induction over intervals [t_l,t_{l+1}]. Assuming a positive-probability event on which the past coordinate error up to t_l is at most ~ε t_l and the coordinate at t_l is within ~ε of target, choose γ in the support of the conditional law of ⟨Z_S(t_l),e_i⟩ on that event. Let h=⟨f_{l+1},e_i⟩ and η=h−γ. Choose s∈(t_l,t_{l+1}) close to t_l so that the contribution of the short interval [t_l,s] to the L²-in-time error is small. Lemma 3 gives a positive-probability event on which the impulsive integral over [t_l,s] produces approximately η with controlled partial supremum. A second application of Lemma 3 gives a positive-probability event on which the subsequent noise over [s,t_{l+1}] remains small. These events are independent of the past and of each other, so their intersection has positive probability. On this intersection, explicit estimates using the semigroup factor e^{−π²i²(t−u)} show that the coordinate stays within the required tolerance on [t_l,t_{l+1}] and ends within tolerance at t_{l+1}. Induction proves the coordinate tracking statement. Independence across coordinate noises and the positive-probability high-mode tail event yield the full L² path and terminal approximation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4: heat equation with impulsive noise has e-property, weak irreducibility, and uniqueness when L_g≤π²
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider on L²([0,1]) the stochastic heat equation with Dirichlet boundary conditions driven by the impulsive noise of Section 3: ∂_t u(t,x)=Δu(t,x)−g(u(t,x))+∂_tZ(t,x), u(t,0)=u(t,1)=0, u(0)=ξ. Here g:R→R is continuously differentiable with bounded derivative and Lipschitz constant L_g, and the associated mild solution defines a Feller semigroup P_tf(x)=E f(u_x(t)). If L_g≤π², then (P_t) has the e-property and is weakly topologically irreducible. Consequently it admits at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the e-property, note that G=Δ−g is weakly dissipative when L_g≤π². Indeed, the Dirichlet Laplacian gives ⟨Δw,w⟩≤−π²∥w∥² by the Poincare inequality, while the Lipschitz bound gives |⟨g(u)−g(v),u−v⟩|≤L_g∥u−v∥²; hence ⟨G(u)−G(v),u−v⟩≤(L_g−π²)∥u−v∥²≤0. Lemma 2 therefore yields the e-property. For irreducibility, write u_ξ(t)=v_ξ(t)+Z_S(t), where Z_S is the stochastic convolution and v_ξ is the adapted solution of the random deterministic equation v_ξ(t)=S(t)ξ+∫_0^tS(t−s)g(v_ξ(s)+Z_S(s))ds, as displayed in the paper. It is enough to show supp Law(u_ξ(T))=L² for arbitrary T>0 and ξ∈L². Fix a target υ∈L² and ε>0. By Proposition 2.11 of Maslowski cited in the paper, there exists f∈L²([0,T];L²) such that the deterministic solution v^f_ξ driven by f satisfies ∥v^f_ξ(T)∥<ε/2. Since g is Lipschitz, the map f↦v^f_ξ(T) is continuous from L²([0,T];L²) to L², so there exists r>0 such that ∫_0^T∥f(t)−h(t)∥²dt<r implies ∥v^h_ξ(T)∥<ε/2. Lemma 4 gives positive probability that Z_S is within r of f in L²([0,T];L²) and that Z_S(T) is within ε/2 of υ. On this event, u_ξ(T)=v^{Z_S}_ξ(T)+Z_S(T) lies within ε of υ. Thus every open ball in L² has positive transition probability from every initial condition, which is stronger than weak topological irreducibility. Proposition 3 then gives uniqueness of the invariant measure if one exists.

proof_techniques.name: Uniqueness via e-property plus weak topological irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces strong Feller smoothing by the e-property, a uniform-in-time equicontinuity condition for P_tφ when φ is bounded Lipschitz. The abstract route is: prove that distinct ergodic invariant measures of an e-process have disjoint supports; prove that weak topological irreducibility and Feller lower semicontinuity force any two ergodic invariant measures to have a common support point; conclude uniqueness. This is weaker than requiring every P_t to map all bounded measurable functions to continuous functions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, a direct strategy is to verify the e-property of its Markov semigroup on C_0([0,1]) and a weak topological irreducibility/support property. Theorem 2 would then prove at most one invariant probability measure without needing a full strong Feller theorem.

proof_techniques.name: Generated-measure argument from support points
proof_techniques.description: A point x generates a measure μ if μ lies in the weak closed convex hull of the Cesaro averages Q_tδ_x. The paper proves that every support point of an ergodic invariant measure generates that measure. The proof combines an accessibility lemma from support points, a submeasure approximation of the invariant measure by images of measures localized near x, and a local characterization of generation using the e-property.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If two candidate invariant measures for the original SPDE can be shown to share an accessible point, this mechanism can identify both as limits generated by the same initial condition. It is useful when direct comparison of invariant measures is hard but support/accessibility information is available.

proof_techniques.name: Tight Cesaro averages plus Arzela-Ascoli from e-property
proof_techniques.description: For x in the tightness set T, the paper proves convergence of Q_tδ_x by contradiction. Tightness provides a compact set carrying almost all mass for all times, while the e-property makes time-averaged observables t^{-1}∫_0^tP_s f ds equicontinuous on that compact set. Arzela-Ascoli then gives uniform convergence along subsequences, and invariance of subsequential limits plus an averaging-shift estimate rule out two different limits.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests a way to analyze long-time averages from a fixed initial condition of the original SPDE if tightness can be proved. Even if existence of invariant measures is assumed separately, the same compact/equicontinuity logic underlies uniqueness.

proof_techniques.name: Synchronous additive-noise coupling for the e-property
proof_techniques.description: For additive-noise evolution equations, the paper couples two solutions with different initial data by using the same noise realization. The difference then solves a deterministic evolution equation. If the deterministic drift is weakly dissipative, the squared Hilbert norm of the difference is nonincreasing in expectation, yielding a uniform Lipschitz bound |P_tf(x)−P_tf(y)|≤L_f∥x−y∥ for Lipschitz f.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original stochastic heat equation, one can try to establish an analogous contraction or nonexpansion estimate for two solutions driven by the same space-time white noise, perhaps first for smooth drift approximations and then by passage to the singular drift. Such a bound would imply the e-property.

proof_techniques.name: Support/irreducibility through controllability of the stochastic convolution
proof_techniques.description: In the heat-equation application, weak irreducibility is proved by showing that the stochastic convolution has sufficiently large support: with positive probability it approximates any prescribed L² time path and terminal point. Continuity of the deterministic solution map with respect to the forcing path then transfers this support property to the full nonlinear equation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original equation has additive space-time white noise, whose stochastic convolution is expected to have rich support in suitable path spaces. A solver could combine a support theorem for the stochastic convolution with continuity of the solution map or approximation by regular drifts to prove weak topological irreducibility on C_0([0,1]).

proof_techniques.name: Feller lower semicontinuity transfers pointwise reachability to invariant-measure support
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 2 uses the fact that for an open set A, Feller continuity implies lower semicontinuity of x↦π_t(x,A). Thus if π_t(x,A)>0 at a support point x of an invariant measure μ, then π_t(z,A)>0 on a neighbourhood of x with positive μ-mass; invariance gives μ(A)>0. This is how weak topological irreducibility produces an actual common support point.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When applying the criterion to the original SPDE, it is not enough to show that each initial condition can reach neighbourhoods with positive probability; one also needs the Feller property, or an equivalent lower-semicontinuity mechanism, to move from pointwise reachability to support intersection of invariant measures.

proof_techniques.name: Borderline dissipativity via the Poincare inequality
proof_techniques.description: In the heat-equation application, the deterministic part Δ−g is weakly dissipative when the Lipschitz constant of g is at most the first Dirichlet eigenvalue π². The estimate is ⟨Δw,w⟩≤−π²∥w∥² and |⟨g(u)−g(v),w⟩|≤L_g∥w∥². This handles the borderline case L_g=π², where strict dissipativity is unavailable.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the singular drift or its approximations can be interpreted as having a one-sided Lipschitz bound compatible with the Dirichlet Laplacian, this technique could yield the e-property even without strict contraction. The original drift is distributional, so this would likely require an approximation or monotonicity argument.

other_useful_info: Throughout the abstract uniqueness theory the paper assumes the semigroup is Feller and stochastically continuous; these are not optional in the stated proofs. The paper recalls from earlier work that T={x:{Q_t(x,·)} is tight} is nonempty if and only if an invariant measure exists, and that the support of any invariant measure is contained in T. The main theorem proves uniqueness only, not existence. The introduction cites Hairer-Mattingly for asymptotic strong Feller uniqueness methods and Komorowski-Peszat-Szarek/Lasota-Szarek for e-process criteria; it also notes a gradient-type sufficient condition for the e-property from prior work: bounds of the form |∇P_tφ(x)|≤C(∥x∥)(sup|φ|+sup∥∇φ∥), uniformly in t, imply the e-property. In the SPDE application, full support of transition laws is used as a stronger condition than weak topological irreducibility.
why_relevant: This paper refines uniqueness criteria based on asymptotic strong Feller/e-property ideas and applies them to nonlinear heat equations with noise. It is useful for proving at most one invariant measure from weaker continuity plus irreducibility assumptions.
